title: Knit and purl: the foundation stitches
subtitle: The two operations every knitted fabric is built from.
excerpt: The knit stitch and the purl stitch are the whole craft. Stocking stitch is knit on one side and purl on the other; garter is knit on every row; rib alternates the two across the same row. Learn these two and the rest of knitting opens up.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from three public-domain references and cross-checked against the standard modern knitting reference:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting, opening pages of the first series (1880s), Internet Archive — the canonical Victorian introduction to the two stitches. Project Gutenberg also holds digital facsimiles of later Weldon's volumes.
• Mary Thomas's Knitting Book (1938), §The Two Fundamental Stitches — Thomas frames knit and purl as one operation on two sides of the work, which is the framing this entry follows.
• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), §Plain knitting — the entry covering the two stitches sits before any pattern in the book.

glossaryTerms.definition: The side of the knitted fabric that faces outward on the finished piece. Pattern instructions mark it as (RS) so the reader knows which way the work is facing.
The side of the knitted fabric that faces inward on the finished piece. Pattern instructions mark it as (WS).
The yarn-throwing knitting style. The right hand wraps the working yarn around the needle on each stitch.
The yarn-picking knitting style. The left hand holds the yarn and the right needle picks the strand on each stitch.

content.text: The knit stitch and the purl stitch are the whole craft. Every knitted fabric on every needle in every chair on every continent is a sequence of these two operations. Stocking stitch (US: stockinette) is knit on one side and purl on the other. Garter is knit on every row. Rib alternates the two across the same row. Cables, lace, and colourwork are all built on the same pair.
Pattern instructions abbreviate them as
k
(knit) and
p

(purl). A row reading "k2, p2 across" means knit two, purl two, repeat to the end. This entry covers both stitches in both knitting styles. Once you can work the two on demand, you can read most patterns.
What you need

A small ball of DK or aran-weight yarn in a smooth, light-coloured fibre — the stitches are clearer when you can see them. A pair of 4 mm (UK 8 / US 6) or 5 mm (UK 6 / US 8) straight needles, whichever sits comfortably in your hand. Wooden or bamboo needles grip the yarn better than metal for learners; choose what feels right.

Cast on 20 stitches using whichever cast-on method you know. The long-tail cast-on is the standard if you're choosing fresh; the knitted cast-on is simpler and uses only the needles. Either gives you a row to work the two stitches across.
The knit stitch
The knit stitch sits on the front of the work as a smooth V. It's the side most people picture when they picture knitting.
In
English style

, hold the needle with the stitches in your left hand, the empty needle in your right, and the working yarn in your right hand behind the work. Insert the right needle into the front of the first stitch on the left needle from left to right, so the needles cross behind the loop. Wrap the working yarn around the right needle anti-clockwise from behind, pull that wrap through the loop on the left needle, and slip the original loop off the left needle. One knit stitch made.
In
Continental style

, the working yarn sits over the left index finger and tensions through the left hand. Insert the right needle the same way, then dip the tip down and pick the strand from under the yarn, pulling it through the loop. The hand motion is smaller and faster for many knitters; the finished stitch is the same.
The purl stitch
The purl stitch sits on the front of the work as a horizontal bump. It's the reverse of the knit stitch from the other side: a row of purls seen from behind looks exactly like a row of knits.

In English style, bring the working yarn to the front of the work between the needles. Insert the right needle into the front of the first stitch on the left needle from right to left, so the needles cross in front of the loop. Wrap the yarn around the right needle anti-clockwise from the front, pull that wrap through the loop, and slip the original loop off. One purl stitch made.

In Continental style, the yarn is already to the front because that's the resting position for Continental purling. Insert the right needle the same way, then push the working yarn down with the left index finger and pick the strand from above, pulling it through the loop. Continental purling has a small extra movement compared to Continental knitting; many knitters find it less natural. Stick with it for ten rows before deciding.
What the two stitches build

Knit every row across both sides and you get garter stitch — bumpy ridges on both faces, identical front and back. Garter is the first-project fabric for almost every learner because the front and back look the same and you don't have to track which side is facing.

Knit one row, purl the next, alternating across both sides and you get stocking stitch — smooth V columns on one side, horizontal bumps on the other. The smooth side is the right side (RS); the bumpy side is the wrong side (WS). Stocking stitch is the fabric of almost every shop-bought jumper.

Alternate knit and purl within the same row and you get rib. 1×1 rib is k1, p1 across; 2×2 rib is k2, p2 across. Rib pulls in on itself and forms the stretchy fabric of every cuff and brim. On the next row you knit the knits and purl the purls as they appear — the rib stays vertical.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong, and why
attrs.intro: These are the snags every learner runs into at least once. None of them are difficult to fix once you can see what's happened.

items.symptom: The piece gets wider as you knit
items.cause: You're picking up an extra stitch at the start of each row by knitting into the slipped first stitch and then knitting the loop hanging off the needle as a separate stitch.
items.fix: Pull the first stitch firmly before you knit it. Check the stitch count at the end of every row — 20 cast on means 20 at the end of row 1, 20 at the end of row 2, every time.

items.symptom: The piece gets narrower as you knit
items.cause: You're letting the last stitch on the row slip off the needle without working it.
items.fix: Slow the last stitch. The yarn pulls firm against the edge as you finish each row; that's the cue to check you've worked all 20.

items.symptom: Stitches sit twisted on the needle
items.cause: You're wrapping the yarn the wrong way around the right needle when forming the stitch — clockwise instead of anti-clockwise.
items.fix: Wrap anti-clockwise from behind on knits and anti-clockwise from the front on purls. The stitch should sit with its right leg in front of the needle.

items.symptom: Purl rows are looser than knit rows
items.cause: Most new knitters tension less firmly on purls than on knits, producing rowing — visible horizontal stripes of looser fabric on the wrong side.
items.fix: Pull the working yarn one tug tighter at the end of each purl. The fabric evens out with practice; in the meantime, blocking the finished piece softens the difference.

items.symptom: The work twists around the needle
items.cause: The cast-on row twisted before you started row one. Common on the long-tail cast-on if you've worked a few rows before noticing.
items.fix: There's no fix once a few rows are in. Undo back to the cast-on, lay the work flat with all the cast-on loops sitting on the same side of the needle, then start row one again.

content.text: Where you'll see these next

Once the two stitches feel automatic, the natural next project is a garter-stitch scarf — knit every row, no purling, until the scarf is the length you want. After that, a 2×2 ribbed hat introduces knitting in the round and the first simple shaping with k2tog. From there the library opens up: cables, lace, colourwork, garments. All of them are made of these two stitches in different orders.
